text: Today I'll be presenting a robotic tap approach of a superior lumbar hernia repair. This is my first lumbar hernia repair. According to the literature, there's only within three hundred or so cases that have been reported since like the seventeen hundreds, which seems very low, especially with all the presentations that are placed onto IHC, but I was pretty excited to see this. So this is a fifty-five-year-old male who presented to me with both an umbilical bulge and a left lateral wall bulge. He was more symptomatic from the umbilical bulge, but I knew that I didn't want to fix the umbilical hernia first before I tackled this repair, so I told him that we would fix the lumbar hernia first and then once he had healed up we can do the umbilical hernia.

The CT scan reveals a fat-containing superior lumbar hernia and a small fat-containing umbilical hernia. The lumbar hernia sac measures approximately 6 by 3 cm and contains retroperitoneal fat. The hernia is bordered by the twelfth rib, internal oblique, and quadratus lumborum, with the latissimus dorsi muscle forming the roof. In the intraoperative image, the diaphragm fibers are visible in the upper left, with transversalis fascia on the right. The hernia defect is small, with underlying iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves, and nearby structures include the twelfth subcostal nerve and lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. Retroperitoneal fat is medialized, and the quadratus lumborum is seen in the lower right corner.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position for the procedure. The surgeon uses the OptiView technique to access the abdomen, placing the first port a hand's breadth away from the subcostal margin and two additional ports eight centimeters apart, triangulating towards the left upper quadrant. The preoperatively marked hernia area is left exposed for external pressure application. The surgeon begins creating a flap, revealing the diaphragm muscle, while extra peritoneal fat is removed to improve visualization of the muscle.

The surgeon's assistant applies external pressure on the abdomen, revealing the hernia's lower position, prompting the surgeon to extend the flap dissection. The transversalis muscle is visible with intact fascia, and the transition to the diaphragm is noted by the change in muscle fiber orientation. The surgeon adjusts the bed flexion to facilitate flap dissection, ensuring the patient is not in a flexed position during hernia closure.

The surgeon approaches the hernia site, revealing a significant amount of retroperitoneal fat and the quadratus lumborum muscle. The surgeon exercises caution to avoid injuring nearby nerves, including the subcostal, ilioinguinal, and iliohypogastric nerves, while reducing the hernia. Downward traction is applied, and electrocautery is used to manipulate the tissue around the hernia defect, which appears smaller than anticipated.

The surgeon uses a zero-degree scope to improve visualization of the hernia, with the assistant applying external pressure to help reduce the retroperitoneal fat. The surgeon manipulates the fat with the left hand, revealing the iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves. The hernia defect measures approximately one by two centimeters, filled with light yellow fat. The surgeon transitions back to a thirty-degree scope, using a retraction tool to maintain exposure while dissecting Dorotis Fascia and medializing the kidney.

The surgeon and assistant identify the plane for mesh placement, aiming for at least five centimeters inferior to the hernia for adequate overlap. The psoas muscle becomes visible as the surgeon measures the hernia, which is approximately one by two centimeters. A heavyweight barred fifteen by fifteen centimeter polypropylene mesh is selected. The surgeon cleans the inferior side to ensure clear visualization of the fascia before closing the defect with a zero absorbable V-lock suture using a large GS-II needle.

The surgeon introduces a fifteen-centimeter heavyweight polypropylene mesh through an eight-millimeter inferior port, encountering difficulty due to the mesh's size. A small tear in the mesh is visible, but the surgeon decides to secure it superiorly to the transversalis fascia to prevent clamshelling and recurrence. The retroperitoneal fat and kidney are repositioned to ensure proper mesh placement, while the surgeon checks the location of the hernia defect and nearby nerves before placing stay sutures.

The surgeon places sutures superiorly and a figure-eight suture in a small tear in the heavyweight mesh, which is positioned over the quadratus lumborum and psoas muscles. The mesh follows the curve of the abdominal wall, and the surgeon ensures that retroperitoneal fat is removed to prevent it from slipping under the mesh. The mesh is laid down, and the kidney and retroperitoneal fat naturally hold it in place. Two six-inch 3-0 V-lock sutures are used to close the peritoneal flap, starting caudally and then superiorly, meeting in the middle. The peritoneum appears healthy, allowing for easy closure without holes.

But that completes the case. Thank you so much for watching. This was a fun case for me. I don't get to see this anatomy a lot. And early on in my career, so excited to get a lumbar hernia and hopefully I'll get some more and I'll be able to show my progress here. But I really want to thank Dr. David Laurier and Dr. Conrad, Dr. Conrad Ballester and Dr. Sabrina Drexel for their videos that they've posted before because they helped prepare me for this case and kind of gave me the confidence to do this case. So thanks everybody. I appreciate your critiques and comments.
